text: Cleavage of C–C bonds in alcohols and their complete oxidation to CO 2 are among the bottlenecks in developing direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) that can be efficiently powered by renewable energy sources. Here, we demonstrated a facile strategy to synthesize core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) consisting of stepped Pt clusters supported on ultrathin (2.3 nm), single-crystal, and stepped Au NWs. The as-obtained Au/Pt NWs behave as well as the Au/Pt NWs treated in a H 2 atmosphere catalyzed the oxidation of alcohols efficiently. Notably, the catalytic performance of Au 1.0 /Pt 0.2 NWs with the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (approximately 75%) reached a mass activity of 196.9 A/mg Pt (32.5 A/mg Pt au ) toward ethanol oxidation, which is the highest value reported for DAFCs. In addition to ethanol, other primary alcohols, such as methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol, were also catalyzed efficiently on the Au/Pt NWs. The structural evolution of the catalysts was identified by in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and the reaction intermediates were identified by in situ infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IRRAS). Collectively, these results demonstrate that the synergy between low-coordinated sites on Pt and the stepped Au core of the NWs is crucial for achieving high activity toward the catalytic oxidation of alcohols. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the under-coordinated Pt-Au surface can greatly reduce the energy barrier for the cleavage of C–C bond in alcohols. Therefore, our results provide a general design strategy for the preparation of core/shell catalysts with stepped surfaces for the efficient catalytic oxidation of alcohols and development of high-performance DAFCs.
